GlobalPolicy.module-level-focus: Fast deterministic checks close to the module using pytest -m unit.
GlobalPolicy.wave-level-focus: Integration checks for adapter wiring and endpoint behavior using pytest -m integration.
GlobalPolicy.phase-level-focus: Full regression suite including architecture boundary checks using pytest.
GlobalPolicy.redaction: Never log secrets, credentials, bearer tokens, or raw audio payloads.

VF-001.NAME: SyncTTSHappyPath
VF-001.scenario: API client submits a valid custom TTS request through the HTTP adapter and receives audio.
VF-001.expected-outcome: WAV audio bytes are returned with 200 status, request context is preserved, and route-level inference markers are emitted.

VF-002.NAME: CloneTTSHappyPath
VF-002.scenario: API client submits a valid clone request with reference audio and receives cloned audio.
VF-002.expected-outcome: Reference audio is normalized when needed, clone synthesis completes, and the response returns WAV audio.

VF-003.NAME: AsyncJobLifecycle
VF-003.scenario: Client submits an async job, polls status, and retrieves the completed audio result.
VF-003.expected-outcome: Job ID is returned immediately, status transitions through queued/running/terminal states, and result retrieval returns audio bytes.

required-signals.trace-sequence: POST /v1/audio/speech/jobs -> queued snapshot -> running or succeeded snapshot -> GET /api/v1/tts/jobs/{job_id}/result returns audio payload

VF-004.NAME: BackendAutoselection
VF-004.scenario: Runtime starts with explicit or autoselect backend configuration and resolves an operational backend.
VF-004.expected-outcome: BackendRegistry chooses a usable backend and ModelRegistry readiness reporting exposes selected backend and model availability.

required-signals.trace-sequence: BackendRegistry chooses explicit or autoselected backend -> ModelRegistry readiness_report exposes selected backend and available models

VF-005.NAME: PlannedRequestToPlanFlow
VF-005.scenario: A normalized synthesis request is resolved through planner, family adapter, runtime model registry, and backend execution without transport-specific branching.
VF-005.expected-outcome: Planner produces an execution plan, the family adapter prepares backend execution inputs, and the coordinator returns a persisted GenerationResult while preserving observability markers.

required-signals.trace-sequence: Synthesis coordinator normalizes request -> planner resolves capability and backend -> runtime model registry provides handle -> backend executes -> coordinator persists output

VF-006.NAME: PlannedHostAwareReadiness
VF-006.scenario: Runtime startup exposes host-aware readiness that distinguishes host, backend, catalog, artifact, and runtime states with explicit rejection reasons.
VF-006.expected-outcome: Readiness payload explains why candidates are accepted or rejected and no longer relies solely on platform-order heuristics.

required-signals.trace-sequence: Host probe captures runtime features -> backend diagnostics collected -> catalog and artifact states evaluated -> readiness report exposes accepted and rejected candidates with reasons

VF-007.NAME: SecondFamilyOnboarding
VF-007.scenario: A second model family is added without transport adapter rewrites and is resolved through the same planner and backend contracts.
VF-007.expected-outcome: Second-family tests pass, planner integration remains generic, and transport adapters remain untouched except for any optional product-surface exposure explicitly approved later.

required-signals.trace-sequence: New family adapter registered -> planner resolves family through existing capability contracts -> integration tests pass without adapter boundary regressions

scenarios.scenario-1: Default settings use local runtime defaults for job backends, auth, manifest path, preload policy, rate limiting, and quota.
scenarios.scenario-2: Environment variables override manifest, preload, auth, rate-limit, and quota settings with normalized values.
scenarios.scenario-3: Explicit models, outputs, voices, and upload staging directories are parsed into the resulting settings object.

module-checks.check-1: python -m pytest tests/unit/core/test_backend_registry.py tests/unit/core/test_job_execution.py tests/unit/core/test_mlx_backend.py tests/unit/test_telegram_bot/test_audio_sender_client.py -m unit

scenarios.scenario-1: Backend selection and model affinity failures surface typed BackendNotAvailableError, BackendCapabilityError, and ModelNotAvailableError cases.
scenarios.scenario-2: Conflicting async job idempotency reuse raises JobIdempotencyConflictError.
scenarios.scenario-3: MLX runtime loading failures surface ModelLoadError with detailed diagnostic context.
scenarios.scenario-4: Telegram audio conversion failures surface AudioConversionError without hiding the root cause.

module-checks.check-1: python -m pytest tests/unit/core/test_tts_service.py tests/integration/test_api.py -m "unit or integration"

scenarios.scenario-1: Structured request logs include request_id, path, status, and endpoint context for HTTP flows.
scenarios.scenario-2: Timeout and worker-failure logs preserve request_id and inference_operation context.
scenarios.scenario-3: TTS service structured logs include mode, text length, language, and persistence context for clone synthesis.

scenarios.scenario-1: Execution metrics collect submitted, started, completed, and queue-depth counters through the local job manager lifecycle.
scenarios.scenario-2: MLX metrics collect cache hits, cache misses, load durations, and load failures.
scenarios.scenario-3: Server readiness reporting exposes the operational metrics snapshot from the runtime registry.

scenarios.scenario-1: Async job submissions preserve operation, request_id, owner principal, and execution timeout metadata.
scenarios.scenario-2: InMemoryJobExecutor dispatches command DTOs across custom, design, and clone operations.
scenarios.scenario-3: VoiceCloneCommand and GenerationResult remain compatible with the TTS service clone flow and structured log assertions.

scenarios.scenario-1: Manifest metadata and artifact validation rules remain consistent with the currently supported backends.
scenarios.scenario-2: Model manifest loading preserves rollout preference and enabled-model filtering semantics.

scenarios.scenario-1: Normalized synthesis capability and execution-plan contracts preserve enough information to drive planner, family, and backend execution.
scenarios.scenario-2: Compatibility wrappers from current command DTOs preserve existing transport-facing behavior.

scenarios.scenario-1: Host probe distinguishes OS, architecture, runtime dependencies, and accelerator hints relevant to backend planning.
scenarios.scenario-2: Missing runtime features are exposed as diagnostics rather than silently folded into model availability.

scenarios.scenario-1: Family adapters match capabilities and translate normalized requests into backend execution inputs without transport involvement.
Current Qwen3 custom, design, and clone semantics are preserved after extraction into an explicit family adapter.
scenarios.scenario-2: Family-specific metadata no longer needs to live in generic catalog surfaces except for compatibility wrappers that are intentionally documented.

module-checks.check-1: python -m pytest tests/unit/core/test_backend_registry.py tests/unit/core/test_mlx_backend.py tests/unit/core/test_qwen_fast_backend.py tests/unit/core/test_model_manifest.py -m unit

scenarios.scenario-1: BackendRegistry honors explicit backend selection and autoselection preference ordering.
scenarios.scenario-2: Unsupported mode, missing local artifacts, and backend-affinity mismatches raise typed capability or availability errors.
scenarios.scenario-3: Manifest artifact validation rules match the runtime requirements expected by MLX, accelerated Qwen fast, and Torch backends.
scenarios.scenario-4: Listed preload policy preloads requested models and exposes cache diagnostics through the registry surface.
scenarios.scenario-5: MLX normalized runtime loading fails fast with ModelLoadError when tokenizer initialization is incomplete.
scenarios.scenario-6: Accelerated Qwen backend exposes explicit readiness rejection reasons and preserves custom-only scope when its fast prerequisites are missing.

scenarios.scenario-1: LocalInMemoryJobStore creates, transitions, cancels, purges, and replays async job snapshots deterministically.
scenarios.scenario-2: LocalBoundedExecutionManager executes jobs to success, handles timeouts, rejects queue overflow, and enforces cancellation rules.
scenarios.scenario-3: LocalJobArtifactHandler removes staged input files during terminal cleanup.
scenarios.scenario-4: Local rate limiting and quota guards remain principal-scoped and deterministic under parallel contention.

scenarios.scenario-1: ModelRegistry readiness_report reflects listed preload policy, loaded model ids, and cache diagnostics.
scenarios.scenario-2: Readiness reporting marks preloaded models as loaded in per-item preload metadata.
scenarios.scenario-3: Readiness reporting exposes fast-lane route candidates and fallback reasons for Qwen custom models without contaminating design/clone modes.

scenarios.scenario-1: Catalog metadata distinguishes stable public model IDs from local artifact folder paths.
scenarios.scenario-2: Catalog metadata can represent family-aware capabilities without regressing current Qwen3 manifest support.

scenarios.scenario-1: Artifact registry distinguishes declared catalog entries from installed and loadable artifact layouts.
scenarios.scenario-2: Missing artifacts surface as explicit artifact-state failures rather than as missing catalog entries.

scenarios.scenario-1: Runtime model registry owns preload and cache state independently from catalog metadata.
scenarios.scenario-2: Runtime model registry exposes loaded, runtime-ready, and preload state clearly in readiness surfaces.

scenarios.scenario-1: Capability resolver exposes why a family, backend, or host combination is accepted or rejected.
scenarios.scenario-2: Readiness surfaces can show capability-based rejection reasons without transport-specific logic.

scenarios.scenario-1: Planner resolves normalized requests into family, model, backend, and artifact decisions without breaking current Qwen3 flows.
scenarios.scenario-2: Planner remains orchestration logic only and does not absorb persistence or transport policy responsibilities.

module-checks.check-1: python -m pytest tests/unit/core/test_backend_registry.py tests/unit/core/test_mlx_backend.py tests/unit/core/test_qwen_fast_backend.py -m unit

scenarios.scenario-1: Generic backend execution contracts preserve current Qwen3 behavior through family adapters.
scenarios.scenario-2: Backend diagnostics and cache state remain explicit after backend-interface generalization.
scenarios.scenario-3: Qwen fast backend remains additive, custom-only, and fallback-safe inside the generic execution contract.

scenarios.scenario-1: Clone synthesis passes the reference audio path to backend generation as a normalized string.
scenarios.scenario-2: Explicit language values are normalized before clone synthesis is executed.
scenarios.scenario-3: Structured clone logs include the entry marker and persistence marker with mode, model, and language context.

module-checks.check-1: python -m pytest tests/unit/core/test_tts_service.py tests/integration/test_api.py -m "unit or integration"

scenarios.scenario-1: Coordinator-shaped service preserves inference guard, persistence, and logging while delegating planning and family semantics.
scenarios.scenario-2: Current transport-facing flows remain behaviorally compatible after coordinator refactor.

scenarios.scenario-1: JobExecutionGateway delegates to the store when no manager is configured.
scenarios.scenario-2: JobExecutionGateway delegates submit and cancel operations to the configured manager when present.
scenarios.scenario-3: InMemoryJobExecutor dispatches custom, design, and clone operations to the application service by operation type.

scenarios.scenario-1: build_runtime passes manifest path and preload configuration into backend and registry wiring.
scenarios.scenario-2: Local job artifact, metadata, and execution factories preserve the local default runtime wiring.
scenarios.scenario-3: Unsupported job backend identifiers are rejected by the bootstrap wiring factories.
scenarios.scenario-4: Architecture checks keep bootstrap wiring config-driven and free of adapter-layer drift.
scenarios.scenario-5: Operator self-check outputs selected backend, per-model execution backend, host snapshot, and missing asset evidence without import-path failures.
scenarios.scenario-6: Operator self-check exposes qwen_fast enablement, test-mode overrides, route candidates, and fallback reasons when the accelerated lane is configured.
scenarios.scenario-7: Host-matrix validation automates baseline and simulated qwen_fast lane evidence so support claims stay synchronized with runtime diagnostics.
scenarios.scenario-8: Windows Docker Desktop validation can reuse the same runtime wiring with Linux containers after the container trust store includes the required corporate proxy root certificate.

scenarios.scenario-1: Server and Telegram Docker images build successfully on the current Windows host with Docker Desktop Linux containers.
scenarios.scenario-2: Container trust-store wiring includes the required corporate proxy root certificate so Python HTTPS package installation succeeds behind the daemon proxy.
scenarios.scenario-3: Server compose runtime exposes healthy `/health/live` and `/health/ready` responses while mounted models, outputs, uploads, voices, and ffmpeg remain visible inside the container.
scenarios.scenario-4: Telegram compose runtime completes startup self-checks, verifies Telegram API connectivity with a real token, and enters the healthy polling loop while persisting delivery state in the named volume.

module-checks.check-1: python -m pytest tests/integration/test_api.py tests/unit/server/test_health_routes.py -m "integration or unit"
module-checks.check-4: QWEN_TTS_RUN_SMOKE=1 QWEN_TTS_SMOKE_MODEL_ID=Piper-en_US-lessac-medium QWEN_TTS_SMOKE_EXPECTED_BACKEND=onnx python -m pytest tests/smoke/test_local_runtime.py -m smoke

scenarios.scenario-1: Health live and readiness endpoints expose expected status plus deep readiness diagnostics.
scenarios.scenario-2: OpenAI-compatible, custom, design, and clone HTTP endpoints return audio and propagate request metadata.
scenarios.scenario-3: Async submit, status, result, cancel, and idempotency flows remain deterministic through the HTTP adapter.
scenarios.scenario-4: Unified HTTP error responses cover validation failures, rate limits, timeouts, worker failures, and ownership checks.
scenarios.scenario-5: Request, inference, and response logs include request_id and GRACE block markers for observability assertions.
scenarios.scenario-6: Unsupported family and operation combinations return controlled model_capability_not_supported errors instead of ambiguous runtime failures.
scenarios.scenario-7: Readiness and model discovery payloads expose selected backend, per-model execution backend, routing reasons, and family capability metadata for mixed-family deployments.
scenarios.scenario-8: Fast-lane metadata exposes `qwen_fast` as an explicit custom-only route candidate with deterministic fallback reasons instead of implying clone/design acceleration.
scenarios.scenario-9: Automated smoke validation can start the local server, wait for healthy startup, execute smoke pytest, and stop the runtime cleanly without manual orchestration.
scenarios.scenario-10: Smoke validation remains Qwen custom sync/async capable by default while supporting an explicit Piper smoke model path through the same public HTTP API with ONNX backend assertions.
scenarios.scenario-11: On the current Windows host, Docker Desktop Linux-container deployment starts the server successfully and returns `status=ok` from both `/health/live` and `/health/ready`.
scenarios.scenario-12: Server Docker readiness on the current Windows host confirms mounted `.models`, `.outputs`, `.uploads`, `.voices`, and in-container ffmpeg availability.

scenarios.scenario-1: TelegramSettings.from_env and build_telegram_runtime validate configuration and assemble TelegramRuntime correctly.
scenarios.scenario-2: CommandDispatcher, TTSSynthesizer, PollingAdapter, TelegramSender, and TelegramBotClient remain correctly wired for adapter flows.
scenarios.scenario-3: DeliveryMetadataStore and TelegramJobOrchestrator preserve idempotency, completion polling, and restart recovery semantics.
scenarios.scenario-4: Telegram audio conversion, voice upload formatting, and sender delegation remain contract-compatible.
scenarios.scenario-5: Telegram observability helpers emit structured GRACE event markers and polling metrics remain visible to tests.
scenarios.scenario-6: Telegram live validation can verify Bot API reachability automatically when a real bot token is provided, while remaining opt-in for secretless environments.
scenarios.scenario-7: Telegram live validation can optionally send a validation ping and confirm that a newer matching inbound message update becomes visible through getUpdates for a dedicated test chat.
scenarios.scenario-8: On the current Windows host, Docker Desktop Linux-container deployment starts the Telegram bot successfully, passes startup self-checks, verifies Telegram API connectivity with a real token, and reaches the healthy polling loop.
scenarios.scenario-9: Telegram Docker runtime on the current Windows host keeps shared model/output/upload/voice mounts and the named `/app/.state` volume available to the polling process.

scenarios.scenario-1: CLI runtime composes only core runtime services and the package entrypoint remains a thin wrapper.
scenarios.scenario-2: KeyboardInterrupt exits the CLI cleanly without surfacing spurious runtime errors.
scenarios.scenario-3: CLI documentation accurately states that the interactive menu remains Qwen-oriented while shared runtime metadata surfaces mixed-family support.

Gate-Migration-Phase-0.goal: Freeze migration baseline in canonical GRACE artifacts before implementation seams begin.

required-evidence.evidence-1: docs/development-plan.xml, docs/knowledge-graph.xml, and docs/verification-plan.xml consistently describe migration target modules, critical flows, and verification references.
required-evidence.evidence-2: No source-code architecture changes are mixed into the Phase 0 packet.

Gate-Migration-Phase-1.goal: Planning, request, and family seams land without breaking current adapter behavior.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Normalized execution-plan and planner contracts exist and are covered by tests.
required-evidence.evidence-2: Server, Telegram, and CLI adapter boundaries remain intact.

Gate-Migration-Phase-2.goal: Qwen3 semantics are extracted into a family adapter with no external behavior regression.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Qwen3 custom, design, and clone flows continue to pass through unit and integration verification.

Gate-Migration-Phase-3.goal: The synthesis coordinator replaces central family semantics in TTSService without breaking persistence, logging, or inference guarding.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Planner/coordinator path preserves current transport-facing synthesis behavior.

Gate-Migration-Phase-4.goal: Family-aware model metadata and compatibility paths preserve current Qwen3 manifest support.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Current manifest v1 entries continue to resolve while family-aware metadata becomes representable.

Gate-Migration-Phase-5.goal: Catalog, artifact, and runtime model responsibilities are separated with clear readiness reporting.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Readiness distinguishes declared, installed, loadable, runtime-ready, and loaded model states.

Gate-Migration-Phase-6.goal: Runtime-oriented backend execution contracts preserve current MLX/Torch behavior through family adapters.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Backends no longer expose only Qwen-mode-shaped generic interfaces.

Gate-Migration-Phase-7.goal: Capability-based host-aware planning replaces simple platform-order backend preference.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Readiness shows accepted and rejected backend candidates with explicit reasons.

Gate-Migration-Phase-8.goal: Second-family proof lands without transport rewrites.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Selected second family is recorded before implementation.
required-evidence.evidence-2: Second-family support lands without introducing transport-specific branching in core.

Gate-Migration-Phase-9.goal: Platform support claims are backed by diagnostics, documentation, and available smoke evidence.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Support matrix in docs matches readiness and test evidence.
required-evidence.evidence-2: Windows Docker Desktop evidence covers successful image builds plus live server and Telegram container startup behavior on the validated host.

Gate-Migration-Phase-10.goal: Legacy transitional seams are removed only after the migration architecture is proven.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Final docs and code describe the same stable architecture without stale transitional references.

Gate-Phase-1.goal: Foundation modules have deterministic local checks and basic observability.

required-evidence.evidence-1: All foundation modules (M-CONFIG, M-ERRORS, M-OBSERVABILITY, M-METRICS, M-CONTRACTS) have passing unit tests.

Gate-Phase-2.goal: Core services wire correctly and pass module-local checks.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Backend selection, model registry, and TTS service have passing tests.

Gate-Phase-4.goal: All transport adapters function correctly end-to-end.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Full test suite passes including architecture boundary checks.
